title: Ex-Head of Eng at Instagram: Career Regrets and Learnings | James Everingham
guest_role: Ex-Head of Engineering at Instagram, Netscape veteran, VP Eng at Meta (Novi/dev infra), founder of Guild

sections.title: FBI showed up at his house
Joining Instagram as head of eng
What he is working on now

key_takeaways: Kicked out of Penn State at 18 with a 0.0 GPA for skipping classes while the university simultaneously hired him full-time (first staff hire without a degree in 50+ years) because they were already using his shareware; as staff he took ~150 credits of CS classes free.
As a teenage phone phreaker he wrote Commodore software emulating operator tones (2600 Hz); the FBI traced him — partly because he proudly put his name on the software — and asked him to stop.

Relationships compound across a career: Borland colleagues took him to Netscape, and three Netscape-era colleagues are still with him at Guild five or six companies later. Mentorship advice: don't ask for a mentor, offer help; 'friends come and go, but enemies collect.'

Netscape lesson: superior technology loses to distribution — Microsoft bundled IE free and instantly killed Netscape's $35 browser revenue ('It's not a technology war, it's a distribution war'); when joining a company, understand which war it's fighting and treat job choice like an investment portfolio decision.

Open-sourcing Mozilla (jwz's idea) let dozens of engineers recruit the world against Microsoft's thousands, but required stripping SSL (legally a munition), obfuscating APIs, and hand-censoring profane variable names on printouts with highlighters.

Instagram Stories succeeded by shrinking the team: a small, talent-dense, founder-led group with clear ownership and no cross-org dependencies shipped it in ~3 months; Mike Krieger's value 'do the simple thing first' governed proving a thesis cheaply before scaling.

CTO vs VP of Eng: he and Krieger published their job descriptions to the org — CTO owns technology/roadmap/experiments/external brand, VP Eng owns people/hiring/execution; companies asking for 'a new CTO' almost always describe VP-of-Eng problems.
Instagram's engineers 'IQ'd past' the usual ~100-person communication breakdown until ~150-200 engineers, helped by everyone being a power user; Boomerang was built by an intern in a 3-day hackathon.

Libra/Novi grew to ~3,500 people and spent years re-architecting for regulators who called it 'the platinum version of stablecoin' yet still blocked it; in retrospect he thinks they should have shipped and asked forgiveness. When he finally worked up courage to resign, David Marcus announced his own departure first, and they co-founded Lightspark.

Running Meta dev infra (~1,000 engineers), his team's internal agentic platform DevMate — agents driving tools, not just coding — went viral among 40,000+ engineers, inspiring Guild: a deterministic control-plane governance layer for enterprise AI agents (permissions, observability, circuit breakers, token budgets) needed no matter which model company wins.
Many AI startups are 'building for the future past,' filling gaps LLMs will soon close (Cursor as autocomplete): 'you build a product on a bus stop, not where the end of the line is.'

Management philosophy: micromanage only briefly to fix broken teams; technology is a creative job, so hire people smarter than you, focus them on outcomes, clear blockers, and get out of the way — micromanaging smart people loses them.

Regrets/advice: time is the real scarce commodity, so ensure you're having good days; go where the smart people are (he spent 7 years in Pennsylvania — like an actor never trying Hollywood); max out your 401k from day one; speak up more.

notable_quotes.speaker: James Everingham
notable_quotes.quote: Microsoft's like, watch this. It's not a technology war, it's a distribution war. And we're going to just bundle this and give it away and people will not use your stuff anymore.

notable_quotes.speaker: James Everingham
notable_quotes.quote: If you want to find a mentor, don't go and ask someone, hey, I need a mentor. Go offer them help.

notable_quotes.speaker: James Everingham
notable_quotes.quote: Be sure you're doing what you love and be sure you're having good days, because that's really your scarce commodity.
